sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: The Bachelor of Arts with a major in Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health explores the complex intersections and relationships among food, culture, economics, the environment, justice, labor, policy, and population health. Many of the world’s widespread health challenges are consequences of broken food systems. You’ll study these systems and their impacts on nutrition and health outcomes.

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: Learning Competencies for the BA in Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.rawLine: When you pursue a Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health (FSNH) degree at the University of Washington School of Public Health, you will gain a deep understanding of how food systems—including their environmental, economic, social, and other facets—affect our nutrition and health. The FSNH major is a STEM Bachelor of Arts major that offers graduates the knowledge, problem-solving skills, and leadership abilities to address the complex challenges and opportunities in food systems across diverse contexts and scales. Graduates will be trained in interdisciplinary and systems-based thinking, which can help them to excel in professions within and beyond food systems.
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.reason: inherits graduate requirement context from nearby heading
sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.copyOnlyDebugText: [parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-food-systems-nutrition-and-health Source URL: https://foodsystems.uw.edu/undergraduate/foodsystems/requirements/ Section title: Learning Competencies for the BA in Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health Raw line: When you pursue a Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health (FSNH) degree at the University of Washington School of Public Health, you will gain a deep understanding of how food systems—including their environmental, economic, social, and other facets—affect our nutrition and health. The FSNH major is a STEM Bachelor of Arts major that offers graduates the knowledge, problem-solving skills, and leadership abilities to address the complex challenges and opportunities in food systems across diverse contexts and scales. Graduates will be trained in interdisciplinary and systems-based thinking, which can help them to excel in professions within and beyond food systems. Course codes extracted: none Detected section role: support-metadata Schedulable: no Reason: inherits graduate requirement context from nearby heading Issue: none

sourceSectionFilterAuditRows.sectionTitle: The core courses in the major provide a foundation in food systems and nutrition followed by exploration of the interactions within food systems using multiple methods, tools, and lenses, including policy, modeling, and community engaged scholarship. Building on this broad foundational coursework, a substantial array of upper division courses enables students to develop a depth of knowledge needed to work in food systems, or to advance food systems, nutrition, and health goals through work in a variety of sectors. Throughout the curriculum, students will apply food systems knowledge through various experiential learning activities, including developing practical and critical thinking skills in a culminating capstone experience aimed at solving real-world problems alongside a variety of partners.

[parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-food-systems-nutrition-and-health Source URL: https://foodsystems.uw.edu/undergraduate/foodsystems/requirements/ Section title: Learning Competencies for the BA in Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health Raw line: When you pursue a Food Systems, Nutrition, and Health (FSNH) degree at the University of Washington School of Public Health, you will gain a deep understanding of how food systems—including their environmental, economic, social, and other facets—affect our nutrition and health. The FSNH major is a STEM Bachelor of Arts major that offers graduates the knowledge, problem-solving skills, and leadership abilities to address the complex challenges and opportunities in food systems across diverse contexts and scales. Graduates will be trained in interdisciplinary and systems-based thinking, which can help them to excel in professions within and beyond food systems. Course codes extracted: none Detected section role: support-metadata Schedulable: no Reason: inherits graduate requirement context from nearby heading Issue: none

[parser prerequisite filter audit] Owner id: uw-seattle-food-systems-nutrition-and-health Source URL: https://foodsystems.uw.edu/undergraduate/foodsystems/requirements/ Section title: The core courses in the major provide a foundation in food systems and nutrition followed by exploration of the interactions within food systems using multiple methods, tools, and lenses, including policy, modeling, and community engaged scholarship. Building on this broad foundational coursework, a substantial array of upper division courses enables students to develop a depth of knowledge needed to work in food systems, or to advance food systems, nutrition, and health goals through work in a variety of sectors. Throughout the curriculum, students will apply food systems knowledge through various experiential learning activities, including developing practical and critical thinking skills in a culminating capstone experience aimed at solving real-world problems alongside a variety of partners. requirementCueLines: 10 credits from among courses that represent the breadth of areas that influence food systems:
10 credits from courses that represent the breadth of areas that influence food systems. Choose from:
45 college credits with a minimum 2.00 cumulative GPA
5 credits of qualitative methods. Choose from:
Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis to confirm minimum requirements are met and to ensure the degree can be completed within the University’s satisfactory progress policies.

Approved list of courses that fulfill Interdisciplinary Breadth course requirements for the Food Systems, Health, and Nutrition Major. The Food Systems major requires 15 credits of interdisciplinary breadth courses.
Complete the online application and upload transcripts for non-UW courses. The application to declare the major is always available and admissions are made on a rolling basis.
Experiential Learning . You’ll complete a capstone, which is an opportunity to explore solutions to real-world issues in partnership with a community-based organization.
First-year students and sophomores must complete course requirements before declaring the major.
chooseStatements: 10 credits from courses that represent the breadth of areas that influence food systems. Choose from:
5 credits of qualitative methods. Choose from:

You will choose a minimum of 20 credits of 300- and 400-level courses organized around 4 concentrations as electives. Concentrations, which do not appear on your transcript, allow you to focus electives on broad areas that impact these systems. These concentrations are:
